title: Understanding Dog Coprophagia: Why Dogs Eat Poop and When to Worry
meta_desc: Is your dog eating feces? Learn about coprophagia, why it happens, and when to consult a vet. Urgency Level: Low.
content_html:
TL;DR: Coprophagia (eating stool) is a common dog behavior that is typically not a medical emergency, though it can lead to parasites or indicate underlying health issues. You should contact a veterinarian if your dog also experiences weight loss, vomiting, or changes in appetite.

What is coprophagia and why is my dog eating stool?

Coprophagia is the technical term for the habit of eating feces. This can involve a dog consuming their own waste, the waste of other dogs, or the stool of different species like cats or wildlife. While this behavior is often stomach-turning for pet parents, it is a relatively common occurrence in dogs and can stem from either behavioral or physiological causes.

Is my dog eating poop considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for coprophagia is categorized as Low, meaning it is rarely an immediate medical emergency.
  • The behavior can lead to the transmission of internal parasites or gastrointestinal upset.
  • You should contact your veterinarian if the behavior is accompanied by vomiting, weight loss, or changes in appetite, as these may indicate an underlying malabsorption issue or parasitic infection.

How can taking a photo of the feces help with my dog's veterinary triage?

  • A clear photo of the feces allows the vet to see the consistency and color.
  • It allows the triage team to check for visible signs of parasites, such as tapeworm segments or roundworms.
  • This visual evidence helps the veterinarian determine if the cause is likely nutritional, medical, or strictly behavioral.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Behavioral problems related to ingestion include coprophagia (eating feces). While coprophagia may occasionally have a medical cause, normal maternal behavior includes consumption of feces and urine of young puppies. Additionally, as part of exploratory behavior, many dogs are attracted to and may ingest feces. Medical causes should be excluded first when addressing coprophagia.

Chapter: Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1568)

faqs.question: Can my dog get sick from eating other animals' poop?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs can contract intestinal parasites, such as hookworms or whipworms, and harmful bacteria like Salmonella or Coccidia from consuming the feces of other animals.

faqs.question: Is coprophagia caused by a nutritional deficiency?
faqs.answer: Sometimes. While many cases are behavioral, some dogs eat stool because they are not absorbing enough nutrients from their diet or have a deficiency in specific enzymes.

faqs.question: How can I stop my dog from eating poop?
faqs.answer: Immediate cleanup of waste is the most effective method. You can also consult your vet about stool-repellent food additives or training techniques like 'leave it'.
